title: Welcome to the Job Search Course!
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Welcome to the Job Search Course!

This course is all about starting your job search, and making sure it's as _effective_ and _efficient_ as it can be!

We _cannot_ promise that:

- You'll get a specific type of job in a specific amount of time
- You'll get a job that pays a specific amount of money

What we _can_ promise is that the techniques and strategies in this course will help you:

- Waste less time with applications that go nowhere
- Present your projects and resume in a way that gets more attention
- Turn a higher percentage of applications into interviews
- Avoid common pitfalls that lead to rejections in the interview process

## Prerequisites

Before starting, make sure you have:

1. Completed a career path on Boot.dev (everything aside from this course)
2. Completed a capstone project that you're proud of

## Learning Goals

- Build a job-ready GitHub profile
- Build a professional LinkedIn profile
- Build a resume that will cut through the noise
- Prepare you with a plan for applying, interviewing, networking, and even negotiating compensation

title: Learn, Build, and Apply
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Learn, Build, and Apply

![Strategy](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/lesson_videos/strategy_v_1080p-1080x1920.mp4)

Before we get into the nitty-gritty of portfolio preparation, I want to draw your attention to something critically important:

**Do not stop learning and building** as you apply for jobs!

I've seen so many prospective engineers "complete" their education, be it a degree, an online course, or a traditional boot-camp, and then _stop learning_. They stop building. They stop growing. They spend a few months applying, get discouraged, and then give up completely.

**DO NOT DO THIS.**

Every new bit of effort you put into your projects, resume, and skillset makes your future job search easier. It's a compounding effect that builds over time.

## How Much Time Should I Spend?

I recommend spending _roughly half_ of the time you dedicate toward your "future career" continuing to learn and build, and the other half doing job-search activities.

So, if you have `10` hours each week to spend on your future programming job:

- Spend ~`5` hours per week learning and building projects
- Spend ~`5` hours per week working on your resume, applying, and interviewing

If you spend _all_ your time looking for work, your skills will decay, making the search harder over time.

If you spend _all_ your time learning and building, no one will even know you're looking for work.

title: Provide Signal
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Provide Signal

Your #1 job as a job-seeker is to provide _signal_ to potential employers. Signal is the opposite of noise.

- **Noise**: _Stuff that doesn't matter_. For example, the fact that you enjoy walks on the beach, your high-school landscaping job, or how many sisters you have.
- **Signal**: _Stuff that matters_. For example, the fact that your open-source library has 10,000 downloads, that you've been writing Go for 3 years, and that you understand how a cache might make the employer's site faster.

Hiring managers are looking for signal. **They're looking for reasons to hire you**! They're _also_ looking for reasons _not_ to hire you.

Your goal is to make it as easy as possible for them to find the _signal_ that _proves you're a good fit_.

## Noise (Bad, Don't Do This)

> I love to write code and walk my dogs on the beach. I have a passion for JavaScript, SQL, Python, Go, and Ruby, and I love building applications that are user-friendly. I would love to work for your company and make your team a success.

## Signal (Good, Do This Instead)

> I used to write a lot of JavaScript, but ever since learning Go and building Stackwrapr (a recent personal project in Go) I want to use as much Go as possible in my next full-time role. The telemetry team at SourceChain seems like a perfect fit because I've always thought monitoring and logging creates interesting problems to solve at scale. I even noticed you're using Kubernetes, which I've wanted to have an excuse to use for a while now!

## Always Provide Signal

Whether it's your resume, your cover letter, your LinkedIn profile, your GitHub README, or the way you talk about yourself in interviews, always provide signal.

- Focus on what matters _to the potential employer_, that is, your technical expertise.
- Show personality. Humans want to work with humans.
- Avoid generic statements. If it's something that could apply to anyone, it doesn't provide signal.
- Be specific. "My cowsay library just hit 435 stars" is better than "I have an open source library that people like".

question.question: Which is better?
answers: To present yourself as a unique human being that has experience solving specific technical problems
To copy what everyone else is doing and hit important keywords
question.correctAnswer: To present yourself as a unique human being that has experience solving specific technical problems

title: Pity
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Pity

This might sound a bit harsh, but it's worth pointing out: **no one is looking to do you any favors**. Well, at least _strangers on the internet_ aren't... which is why networking is important and we'll talk about it later.

As a hiring manager, I sometimes get DMs, cover letters or emails sporting a sob story like this:

> I've been trying to get my first programming job for 2 years, and I'm really struggling. Please consider me for this role - it's my last chance before I go back to putting the fries in the bag.

There are two big problems here:

1. Hiring managers know they'll spend a lot of time with whoever they hire, and no one wants to spend time with negative people. This message communicates that you're a negative person.
2. Hiring managers want to hire people that get the work done _well_. This doesn't communicate that you have the skills. In fact, it implies the opposite: that _no one else thinks you can do the job either_.

> [!lane]
> You wouldn't start a date by telling your romantic interest that no one wants to be with you. So why would you start a job application that way? **Exude confidence, don't ask for pity**.

title: Portfolio Projects
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Portfolio Projects

![Interviewing](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/lesson_videos/projects-1080x1920.mp4)

**The single most important section on your resume is your portfolio projects.**

Projects are demonstrable evidence that _you can do what you say you can do_. Admittedly, there _are_ ways to cheat - like generating AI slop projects, or stealing code and trying to pass it off as your own... but for the most part, good hiring managers can tell if you did that after they have the chance to talk to you.

## Projects Should Stand Out

Too many entry-level developers build the same old stuff:

- TODO apps
- CRUD servers
- Spotify clones
- Tic Tac Toe games

These are good projects for _learning_, but they're not _remarkable enough_ to _grab attention_. And grabbing attention is how you stand out in a fat stack of resumes.

> [!lane]
> In college I built a Python script that found "loudness spikes" in an `.mp3` file. The end result was that a user could play a drum beat into a microphone, and get back the sheet music for it.
>
> It _barely worked_, but it was a unique project, and I actually won 2nd place in a local hackathon with it. The point is, your project doesn't have to be _perfect_ or crazily _ambitious_, but it should be _interesting_.

Your project should make people ask follow up questions like:

- "I wonder what technologies they used to build that?"
- "How accurate is the detection? I want to try!"

## Project Types

Generally speaking, there are several main types of projects:

- A command line tool
- A web server
- A web app
- A library
- A game
- A native app (desktop or mobile)

Do _not_ sleep on command line tools and libraries! Many developers think they need to build end-to-end applications to impress hiring managers, but that's not the case. **Just solve an interesting problem, and do it well.**

title: How Many Projects?
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# How Many Projects?

_Most_ developers should feature 1 or 2 projects on their resume, and no more than 3.

It's better to have a couple _great_ projects than a bunch of mediocre projects. And once you've narrowed it down to just a couple, you can focus all of your time on making those ones _incredible_.

Of those, only one will be your **primary project**. It's the one you should put most of your time into, and will be the one listed first on your resume. You'll be lucky if most of the people reading your application even take a serious look at _one_ of your projects, so you want to make sure that time is well spent.

## Assignment

**Choose a primary project.** This is likely your [capstone project](https://www.boot.dev/courses/build-capstone-project) from earlier.

_Submit the link to your primary project._

title: README Section
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# README Section

The most important part of your portfolio project is the `README.md` file found at the root of your repository.

It's the first thing that hiring managers will see because platforms like GitHub and GitLab render the `README.md` file front-and-center on the project's page.

Many hiring managers won't even take the time to _look at the actual code_, let alone clone and run it. They'll read the `README`, and from there decide if you're worth any more of their time.

## Page Structure

A good `README.md` often has these sections:

- Title
- Description
- Motivation
- Quick Start
- Usage
- Contributing

We will look at each of these in detail later, but for now let's work on your _title_.

## Title Section

Give your project a good name! Don't name it "project", "API server", or "test app". Give it a name that's unique and memorable. Personality is _good_. Here are some examples:

- "Text Tunnel": A terminal-based real-time messaging app
- "Steamiest": A web app that tells you which of your Steam friends has the most similar game library
- "slowviz": A library that makes it easy to visualize the network latency of a Go application

Most importantly it should:

- Pique interest
- Hint at what the project does

## Markdown Formatting

Your `README.md` should be a well-formatted [Markdown file](https://commonmark.org/help/). Your title should be an `H1` (heading 1, the largest heading). Subheadings should be `H2`s and `H3`s to organize the rest of your README into a neat hierarchy.

Use rich text to make your README easy to read. Don't be shy of bold, italics, numbered lists, bullet points, and other formatting options. **Skimmability is good!**

## Assignment

_Submit the link to your project's GitHub repo_ after you've added a title with an `H1` heading.

title: Description Section
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Description Section

Your `README`'s project description should come after the title and have 1 or _maybe 2_ sentences about what your project does. State it as simply as possible. For example:

> Zipzod is a command-line tool in Go for zipping and unzipping files with an ultra low memory footprint.

> Wrapper of `rabbitmq/amqp091-go` that provides reconnection logic and sane defaults.
>
> -- [go-rabbitmq](https://github.com/wagslane/go-rabbitmq)

> The fun, functional and stateful way to build terminal apps. A Go framework based on The Elm Architecture. Bubble Tea is well-suited for simple and complex terminal applications, either inline, full-window, or a mix of both.
>
> -- [bubbletea](https://github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea)

## Don't Minimize Your Work

Never minimize your work with language like "just", "toy" or "test". Avoid saying things like:

- This is just a toy app
- This is just a test project
- This is for practice

These imply that you built a toy project _because you don't have the skills_ to build a "real" one. **Take pride in your work!**

## Show Don't Tell

After a short text-based description, add an image, GIF, live link, or video that shows your project in action! Hiring managers ~~are lazy~~ have limited time, so make it _as easy as possible_ for them to see what you built!

[Bubble Tea's repo](https://github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea) has a great example of this:

![Bubble Tea CLI](https://camo.githubusercontent.com/e2b13cfb4ea64e98028ab21e0dca97a6af46d8eeb78ef092c5cd4669ea7b7bef/68747470733a2f2f73747566662e636861726d2e73682f627562626c657465612f627562626c657465612d6578616d706c652e676966)

title: Motivation Section
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Motivation Section

After introducing _what_ your project is, it's time to explain _why_ you built it. Use about one paragraph to describe _why_ someone should care about your project. For example:

> There are a lot of ways to zip and unzip files, but they use so much memory! Zipzod is the zipping tool you need for working on low-memory devices like Raspberry Pis. I use a Raspberry Pi as a home server, and I was frustrated by my inability to easily manage large files on a small device, so I built zipzod.

Add some personal flair and motivation to this section. It should cover why _you chose_ to build it.

## Examples

[HTMX](https://github.com/bigskysoftware/htmx#motivation)

```md
- Why should only <a> and <form> be able to make HTTP requests?
- Why should only click & submit events trigger them?
- Why should only GET & POST be available?
- Why should you only be able to replace the entire screen?

By removing these arbitrary constraints htmx completes HTML as a hypertext
```

[python-websockets](https://github.com/python-websockets/websockets#why-should-i-use-websockets)

```md
The development of websockets is shaped by four principles:

- Correctness: websockets is heavily tested for compliance with RFC 6455. Continuous integration fails under 100% branch coverage.
- Simplicity: all you need to understand is msg = await ws.recv() and await ws.send(msg). websockets takes care of managing connections so you can focus on your application.
- Robustness: websockets is built for production. For example, it was the only library to handle backpressure correctly before the issue became widely known in the Python community.
- Performance: memory usage is optimized and configurable. A C extension accelerates expensive operations. It's pre-compiled for Linux, macOS and Windows and packaged in the wheel format for each system and Python version.
```

## Tell a Story

You're trying to grab attention here, and good stories do that.

> I had problem `A` and I tried `B` but it didn't work because `C`. I built `D` and now I can do `E` with ease!

title: Quick Start Section
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Quick Start Section

If the reader is interested enough in the "what" and the "why", now it's time to show them "how"! This section should have the _minimum number of steps_ they need to take to use your project.

Keep it _as simple as possible_! Here's an example:

````md
## 🚀 Quick Start

### 1. Install zipzod using the Go toolchain

```bash
# Install Zipzod
go install github.com/xyz/zipzod@latest

# Compress an "input" Directory Into an "output.zip" File
zipzod -i ./input -o ./output.zip
```
````

Or, if your project is a deployed web application, it might be even simpler:

```md
## 🚀 Quick Start

1. Navigate to [zipzod.xyz](https://zipzod.xyz) and upload your file!
2. Click the download link when it's done compressing!
```

If your project is hard to use, make it easier! If you've made it as easy as possible and it's still a pain in the butt, be sure that you have a video or GIF or _something_ in addition to the quick start section in case the reader doesn't have time to try it out themselves.

## More Good Examples

- [discordgo](https://github.com/bwmarrin/discordgo#getting-started)
- [discord.py](https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py#quick-example) (broken down into "installing" and "Quick Example" sections)

question.question: The `____` it is to use, the more likely it is to be used

title: Usage Section
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Usage Section

The "usage" section is where you show off the advanced features of your project. Think of this as the larger reference manual, or the comprehensive technical documentation. Assuming the reader has been "hooked" by your what, why, and how, this section should _wow_ them with the full capabilities of your project.

For example:

````md
## 📖 Usage

Available flags:

- `-i` - The input file or directory
- `-o` - The output file or directory
- `-v` - Verbose output
- `-h` - Show help
- `-p` - The number of parallel workers to use (default 4)
- `-d` - The maximum directory depth to recurse (default 6)
- `-f` - The file extensions to include (default .txt, .md)

## Examples

Unzip a file:

```bash
zipzod -i ./input.zip -o ./output
```

Zip with a different number of workers:

```bash
zipzod -i ./input -o ./output.zip -p 8
```
````

title: Contributing Section
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Contributing Section

A "contributing" section helps the reader understand how they can contribute, or more likely in your case, how they can _build and run your project_ for local development.

If a hiring manager is _super_ interested in your project, they might want to pull it down and play with it, and you want to make that as easy as possible for them. For example:

````md
## 🤝 Contributing

### Clone the repo

```bash
git clone https://github.com/xyz/zipzod@latest
cd zipzod
```

### Build the compiled binary

```bash
go build
```

### Run the test suite

```bash
go test ./...
```

### Submit a pull request

If you'd like to contribute, please fork the repository and open a pull request to the `main` branch.
````

## Some Examples of Good READMEs

- [go-rabbitmq](https://github.com/wagslane/go-rabbitmq)
- [bubbletea](https://github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea)
- [Scrapy](https://github.com/scrapy/scrapy)
- [Black](https://github.com/psf/black)

## Assignment

1. [ ] Ensure all the Markdown in your `README` is rendering as you expect on GitHub.
2. [ ] Make sure you have "Motivation", "Quick Start", "Usage" and "Contributing" sections (notice that the checks are looking for these exact terms).

**Submit the GitHub checks.**

title: GitHub Profile
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# GitHub Profile

Now that you have one or two projects _on_ GitHub that you're proud of, let's update your GitHub _profile_ itself. Your GitHub profile probably won't be looked at _quite_ as often as your projects themselves, but it _will_ be looked at, especially when an employer is researching you before an interview.

Here are some examples of good GitHub profile pages:

- [Eddie Jaoude](https://github.com/eddiejaoude)
- [Lane Wagner](https://github.com/wagslane)
- [Daniel Roe](https://github.com/danielroe)
- [Carlos Becker](https://github.com/caarlos0)

## Assignment

**Fill out your GitHub profile's metadata.**

1. Add a **profile picture** of yourself.
   1. Take the time to take a good one if you don't have one already.
   2. Smile. Look like a happy person, not someone that's pissed that they're taking a photo.
   3. Ideally a face shot, not your back as you walk away on the beach.
   4. If you _really_ don't want your picture online, at least add _something_ that will make you seem likely to be a real person. Don't leave the default, and don't use a bored ape NFT.
2. Add your **full name** as it appears on your resume
3. Make your **GitHub handle** (username) something that you're not embarrassed by. Ideally it's just your name or a close variant of it.
4. Add a **short bio**. Try to make it technical and unique to you. You can plug your favorite programming language or technology there if you want.
5. Add your **location**. This will really help when you're applying to local companies.

Simply having most of the fields filled out makes you look more like a real person! Most bots don't take the time.

question.question: You're trying to look like a `____` who `____`
answers: human, puts effort into their work
bot, puts effort into their work

title: Profile README
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Profile README

You can [add a README](https://docs.github.com/en/account-and-profile/setting-up-and-managing-your-github-profile/customizing-your-profile/managing-your-profile-readme) to your profile page by creating a repo with the same name as your GitHub username and adding a `README.md` to _that_ repo. That `README` will render on your profile page. It's driven completely by naming convention. Here's an [example of my repo](https://github.com/wagslane/wagslane) and it renders [here](https://github.com/wagslane).

**Think of your profile README as a short _"about my technical life"_ section.**

Keep it short. At most a paragraph or two, or maybe just a list of bullet points. This is a good place to show off:

- Specific language skills
- Favorite projects you're building
- Open source contributions
- Technical blogs, videos, talks, or content you've created
- Awards or recognitions you've received
- Books you're reading
- Courses you're taking
- Links to other profiles (LinkedIn, Twitter, etc.)

## Examples

> I'm Lane, I write and operate backend systems in Go and Python.
>
> I've been spending a lot of time contributing to a client library for [RabbitMQ](https://github.com/wagslane/go-rabbitmq) recently - I'm interested in how we can increase the allowed functionality while keeping the spirit of "batteries included". I'm looking for a new full time role, so if you know of any companies that need a skilled Go or Python engineer, [reach out!](https://www.linkedin.com/in/wagslane/)
>
> ## Books I'm reading
>
> - [The Pragmatic Programmer](https://pragprog.com/titles/tpp20/the-pragmatic-programmer-20th-anniversary-edition/)
> - [The Phoenix Project](https://itrevolution.com/the-phoenix-project/)
> - [Hypermedia Systems](https://hypermedia.systems/)

> I'm Chad, I've been diving into memory arenas in C and trying to decide whether or not I like Rust's ownership model.
>
> I've written some [blog posts on Medium](https://www.boot.dev/blog/) about my early thoughts on the borrow checker, and I'm currently digging into the [Rust book](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/) when I'm not keeping my kids from eating crayons.

## Assignment

**Submit the link to your GitHub profile README.**

> [!warning]
> Remember, the last two sections of the URL should _match_ because your repo name and username should be the same.

title: Pinning Repos
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Pinning Repos

You can [pin up to 6 repositories](https://docs.github.com/en/github/setting-up-and-managing-your-github-profile/pinning-items-to-your-profile) to your GitHub profile.

You should **absolutely** do this for your 1-3 portfolio projects, and _maybe_ even some of your other projects... but _only_ if you're actually proud of them. Don't pin more projects just to hit that 6 repo limit.

## Assignment

**Pin your portfolio projects to your GitHub profile.**

_Order your pins from most to least important._ Most readers will only click on the first one or two.

question.question: The order of your pinned repos `____`

title: GitHub Heatmap
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# GitHub Heatmap

A great heatmap isn't _required_, but it certainly doesn't _hurt_. If your heatmap looks bare, you should figure out what's wrong in your workflow that's preventing your work from being tracked properly... or you should code more often!

- Are you not committing often enough?
- Are you not pushing often enough?
- Are you not storing all your code in GitHub?
- Is your heatmap not public, or are your settings not showing commits to private repos?

Over time, if you're consistently writing code, your heatmap will fill up and look great. You should **be coding at least 3-4 days out of the week** if you're serious about landing a job. This is what I'd expect the heatmap of a serious developer to look like:

![heatmap](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/kqgOhYq-1280x275.png)

Don't feel bad if you're not there yet - but make the changes you need _now_ so that you're there in a few months!

> [!danger]
> [Vibe coders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vibe_coding) are running rampant, so you _also don't want to have a suspiciously high_ number of contributions. It's not anything to worry about if you're actually writing code, but don't try to fake your contribution graph because employers _are_ more wary of that sort of thing these days.

question.question: Healthy commit and push habits with personal projects results in a `____` heatmap

title: Stars
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Stars

You don't _need_ stars on your GitHub repos, but they can't hurt. When I see that someone has worked on a GitHub repo with more than even `20` stars, I think, "Hey, people are actually using their work!"

[Social proof](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_proof) is a _very_ powerful thing in both marketing _and_ hiring. It sends a _signal_ that other people think your work is valuable. They don't just need to take _your_ word about how awesome you are. So don't underestimate the power of:

- GitHub stars
- Social followers
- Blog readers
- Video views
- Podcast listeners
- Personal recommendations
- etc.

That said, I _would not_ spend too much time "marketing" your projects to try to get stars - but there are a couple of simple things I do recommend:

- Share your projects _at least once_ on LinkedIn/Slack/Reddit/Discord/etc.
- When you share, ask for feedback and suggestions (this makes it less spammy)
- Contribute to _existing_ open-source projects that already have stars and users

title: Resume
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Resume

Your resume is _critically_ important, which hopefully goes without saying.

Write your resume somewhere that's easily accessible. I use Google Docs. The nice thing about something like Google Docs is you can easily give a friend a link to it, and they can drop comments on it and collaborate with you. But the important part is that you have a **source of truth for it, and can update it easily**.

## Sending Your Resume

When you send your resume to an _employer_, I recommend sending it as a PDF for a few reasons:

1. No one will accidentally edit it
2. It can be opened on any device easily (browsers open PDFs these days)
3. It looks the same on every device

Some people have issues with the various `.doc` and `.docx` formats.

![Resume explainer](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/lesson_videos/resume_v-1080x1920.mp4)

## Assignment

Go ahead and open a new blank document and let's get started.

question.question: Edit your resume as `____` and send it to employers as a `____`

title: Template
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Template

It's important (at least in my opinion) for your resume to be _visually appealing and unique_.

Your resume isn't a design contest, but think about it: it's going to sit in a digital stack of 30 other resumes. If the other 29 resumes are black text on white background with 12-point New Roman font, even just adding a splash of color to yours will make it stand out. **Remember, we're all about standing out**.

## Resume Template Examples

Here are a few resume templates that can be a good starting point. Feel free to use one, or to find/create your own. Pick something that you think looks good, and that you think will make you stand out just a little bit.

- [Splash of red](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1W7An1H3mQbtx8J8419LgHxTIbuA-24zmV6H2KLRF6gs)
- [Bit of blue](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1K4DmVd8OKn1pBeqVoDrnMJgiEqKebMRqYXOczF2qxtA)
- [Structured Orange](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1B7tKw2ViDsxMl5ruJxiSpleG0tAuV5bGhn0jvwxQms0)
- [Good with green](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1skTEEymyn0-szPZn_A2cBm84DlRTh4PHki_7b5UQhB8)
- [Monospace](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1T4G3afOykA2es0Icfs81FZin7vVjkDBlkUYE31UeHd8)

> [!warning]

title: Structure
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Structure

Think of your resume as the landing page of your personal website. You only have a few moments to capture the attention of the reader. So make it **impossible to miss the important stuff**.

If you've _never had a job in tech_, I recommend this structure, from top to bottom:

1. Name + contact info
2. About me
3. Skills
4. Portfolio Projects
5. Education
6. Experience

If you _have had a job in tech_, I recommend this structure:

1. Name + contact info
2. About me
3. Skills
4. Experience
5. Education
6. Portfolio Projects

Basically we want your best stuff towards the top. If this is your first job, **your portfolio projects are your best stuff**. If you've had a job in tech, **your work experience is your best stuff**.

## Assignment

**Add those sections to your resume in the correct order.**

Don't worry about what's _in_ each section yet.

title: Contact Information
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Contact Information

**Contact info comes first**.

- Use your full name, and make it prominent.
- Your email is the **next most important thing**. It's best if you have a reasonable-looking email address. For example, `elendil.voronda@gmail.com` is _much_ better than `slayerl0rd69@gmail.com`
- Your phone number
- A _clickable link_ to your GitHub profile.
- A _clickable link_ to your LinkedIn profile.

## A Note on Phone Numbers

Some folks are concerned that they'll get spam calls, spam emails, etc. by listing all this personal info on their resume.

My take? _That's the least of your concerns_. You can block a spam call or email, but you do _not_ want to miss out on a job opportunity because you didn't make it easy for a recruiter to contact you.

## Assignment

**Update your contact information**.

title: About Section
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# About Section

> [!lane]
> I have mixed feelings on the "about me" section/paragraph at the top of resumes. I think it's worth doing if you do it well, but if you do it poorly, it's just a waste of space.

Do _not_ make the mistake of talking about your hobbies or passion for Indian cuisine in this section. Take advantage of these few sentences to **sell your story** and all the **hard work you've put in** to get to this point as a developer.

## Bad Examples

> I'm a developer who writes Go and Python. I love to hang out with my family and write code in my spare time. I'm looking for a great company to work for.

> I have a passion for programming and artisan coffee. I love to learn new things and I'm excited to start my career in software development. I love to play tennis and go hiking on the weekends.

## Good Examples

> I decided to give software engineering a go 3 years ago when I stumbled onto a book about Python and decided to automate the grading of my students' math homework. I was a middle school teacher at the time, but I got hooked on coding and have been learning everything I can about programming ever since. I can't wait to transition to a full-time career in development.

> I got into programming a few years ago when I was helping out at my family's restaurant. We were constantly juggling phone calls, handwritten tickets, and missing orders, so I wrote a little program to track everything using Google sheets and Python scripts. It was so fun (and useful) that I dove deeper into programming and haven't stopped since.

## What's the Difference?

- Be specific. Don't just say you love Go, say _why_, and _which parts_.
- Tell a story, don't list facts. Let them get to know you through the _narrative_.
- Convey that you're smart, capable, and hard-working without saying it outright. **Show it**.

## Assignment

**Write your "about me" section for your resume.**

title: Skills
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Skills

A "skills" section shouldn't take up much room - it's just a _list_ of technologies you're familiar with and technical skills that you have.

Frankly, I don't think it's all that useful to the _hiring manager_, but non-technical recruiters, HR folks, and ATS (Applicant Tracking Systems) do use it in preliminary screening. So it's worth including.

## Structure

I like to use a simple list of bullet points, where each bullet point is a logical grouping of related technologies. For example:

- Go, Python, JavaScript
- PostgreSQL, SQLite, MongoDB
- Docker, GCP, AWS
- Git, GitHub, GitHub Actions, Jenkins

## Don't Try to Qualify Your Skills

I see some resumes that look like this:

- Go (advanced)
- Python (intermediate)
- JavaScript (beginner)

Or this:

- Go 4/5
- Python 3/5
- JavaScript 2/5

**What am I supposed to understand from this as a hiring manager???** What does "3/5" mean??? I have no idea. It's all relative, and it's all nonsense. Just list the stuff you feel comfortable working with, and leave it at that.

## Resume Versions

_It's okay to have multiple versions of your resume_.

When you apply for a full-stack Python job, make sure to _emphasize those skills_. If you're applying for a back-end Go job, again, make sure to emphasize those skills. If you find yourself applying to a few different types of jobs, it's okay to maintain a few different versions of your resume, each tailored to the type of job you're applying for.

When I'm hiring a junior backend Go developer I'd rather see this:

- Go, Docker, Kubernetes, PostgreSQL
- ...

Than this:

- Go, Docker, Kubernetes, PostgreSQL, Java, JavaScript, C# C, C++, Rust, Ruby, PHP, Python, ...

The first is more believable, and it emphasizes the skills that are actually relevant to the job I'm hiring for.

## Assignment

**Add a "skills" section to your resume.**

question.question: You should include a progress bar next to each skill representing your proficiency in that skill.

title: Projects
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Projects

Assuming you're looking for your _first_ job as a programmer, **this is the most important section of your resume**.

It's the section that will get you the interview, and maybe even the job. You should already have your projects listed on your GitHub profile, so this should be easy!

Each project entry should look something like this:

1. **Name of project**. Again, it's best if the names of the projects are unique and memorable!
2. **Link to the project on GitHub**. Make this link clickable.
3. **A short description of the project**. This can be the same as the what/why descriptions on the project's readme.
4. **The tech stack and technologies used** in the project.

- Don't be afraid to use a paragraph or two for each project.
- List 1-2 projects, 3 is the absolute maximum, and 2 is probably the sweet spot.

## Don't Say "Just"

Don't use words like "just", "toy" or "followed a tutorial" to describe your projects. Don't downplay your work. If your work sucks, make it better, then come back and stop downplaying it.

Watch this [~60-second clip](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HYaXz8y1wh0&t=1977s) from the Backend Banter podcast where James Q Quick talks about new developers downplaying their work.

## Examples

Here are some examples of good project entries:

> [go-rabbitmq](https://github.com/wagslane/go-rabbitmq)
>
> I built this wrapper of `rabbitmq/amqp091-go` to provide reconnection logic and sane defaults when working with RabbitMQ. The `amqp091-go` library is currently the most robust and well-supported Go client that I'm aware of. It's a fantastic option, but it's fairly low-level and stays within the scope of the AMQP protocol, as such, few quality of life features exist for RabbitMQ users.
>
> Go-rabbitmq provides most of the nitty-gritty functionality of Streadway's AMQP, but makes it easier to work with via a higher-level API. Go-rabbitmq is also built specifically for Rabbit, not for the more general AMQP protocol.

> [go-tinytime](https://github.com/wagslane/go-tinytime)
>
> I built this library to make it convenient to work with lightweight timestamps in Go. The standard library's `time.Time` type uses ~24 bytes of data in memory. I was working on a project where I needed to store hundreds of thousands of timestamps in memory, and I wanted to reduce the memory footprint of my application. TinyTime uses just 4 bytes of memory per timestamp, and can be converted to and from a standard `time.Time` object.

title: Education
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Education

This section should be fairly straightforward. List your highest level of education, and include any impressive achievements, grades, test scores, or certifications.

- If you have a degree, even if it's not CS-related, list it.
- If you have particularly good grades or impressive test scores, list them. (e.g. ACT/SAT)
- If you have any impressive official certifications, list them. (e.g. AWS Certified Developer Associate)
- List your Boot.dev education!

List Boot.dev on your resume and link to your public Boot.dev profile (make sure it's filled out!). I also recommend adding some explanatory text in case the person reading isn't familiar with the program. Something like this:

> Boot.dev
>
> https://boot.dev/u/wagslane
>
> Dec 2022 - Oct 2023
>
> Successfully completed Boot.dev, a rigorous backend engineering curriculum. I finished over 20 courses and projects, including courses on DSA, functional programming, web servers, and SQL databases to name a few.

question.question: You should leave your education section blank if you have an unrelated bachelor's degree.
answers: Only if you majored in something silly like _frontend_ software engineering

title: Work Experience
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Work Experience

**You don't need to have had a job in tech to have work experience**.

If you've had a job in _any field_, you have work experience, and you should list it. You probably don't need to go into a ton of _detail_ about your role as a Bank Teller, but you should list it.

_Hiring managers still want to know that you're responsible enough to hold down a job._ You can also communicate soft skills like hard work, responsibility, and good communication through your non-coding work experience.

## Getting Some Experience

**The hardest coding job to get is your first one**. Someone has to take a chance on you because you don't have a track record of shipping real code in production. The second job is often 10x easier to get than the first one, at least in my experience.

However, there are some tricks for finagling some work experience, even if you haven't _technically_ had a job in tech before...

## Build for Free

I do _not_ recommend picking up an unpaid internship that requires you to quit a job that's currently paying you - even if it's not coding related. However, maybe you can help a local non-profit or small business with some of their technical needs _in your spare time_.

- Build them a website
- Write them a script that automates part of their workflow
- Build a server that pulls data from their Google Sheets and emails it to them every morning

The point is, if you can build a _real solution_ that someone _actually uses_, you **should** put that on your resume as work experience. It doesn't matter that you weren't paid. We don't list salaries on our resumes anyway.

I love this [~90-second](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0YU4D1TgkrE&t=2105s) clip from the Backend Banter podcast where Bill Kennedy talks about hacking your way into some experience.

## Contribute to Open Source

While not required, this is one of the most under-utilized tools for providing signal to employers that you know what you're doing. If your pull requests have been merged into a popular open-source project that's used in production by real companies, that's a _great_ indication that you know what you're doing. Depending on the size of the contribution it can be _as good as_ or _even better than_ "real" work experience.

The trick is to make sure your contribution is _significant_. Don't just fix a typo in the docs. Add a new feature or fix a bug (and add tests for the bug). Make it something you can proudly list on your resume, and that you're happy to talk about in an interview.

## Assignment

Add a "work experience" section to your resume.

title: LinkedIn Profile
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# LinkedIn Profile

Aside from your GitHub, your [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/) profile is probably your 2nd most important social presence when hunting for jobs. I think of LinkedIn as an _interactive version of my resume_. Remember, you want to:

- Seem like a human who cares about their craft
- Be noticed as a professional who has taken the time to present themselves well
- Make it easy for employers to learn more about you

## Assignment

Take the time to fill out some of the simpler profile details:

1. [ ] Add a profile picture (you should already have a good one from the last chapter)
2. [ ] Add your location
3. [ ] Add a background image. Do you have a picture of you doing something interesting? Maybe at the beach? At a conference? Ideally, it features you, but worst-case scenario you can use something generic like the city skyline where you live or some scenery.
4. [ ] If you have an impressive ACT/SAT/GPA feel free to add it to the test scores section
5. [ ] Join some groups related to your career interests, e.g. "Go Developer Community", "Python Programming", etc. These will show at the bottom of your profile.
6. [ ] If you've participated in any hackathons or won any _relevant_ awards, add them to your profile in that section
7. [ ] Add your 2-3 GitHub portfolio projects to the "projects" section
8. [ ] Add contact info. If you don't have a personal site, just use your GitHub profile.
9. [ ] Fill in the skills section. Max that baby out. Do research on the jobs you're interested in and be sure you list the ones they're looking for. This is also a good time to reflect on any gaps that you can work on filling. If you have any tech-adjacent jobs listed, you can assign skills to those specific jobs as well.
10. [ ] Fill in the "about" section with 1-2 paragraphs. This should look similar to the stuff you added to your GitHub profile README. Again, focus on your technical skills, interests, and projects. You can also drop skill keywords, but I wouldn't overly stuff it, it should look human.

_We'll hit the bigger sections in later lessons_.

### Example LinkedIn Profiles

- [Lane Wagner](https://www.linkedin.com/in/wagslane/)
- [Danny Thompson](https://www.linkedin.com/in/dthompsondev/)
- [Miriah Peterson](https://www.linkedin.com/in/miriah-peterson-35649b5b/)

title: Experience Section
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Experience Section

The experience section of your LinkedIn profile is _critically important_ if you've had any work experience at all.

## Non-Technical Experience

If you've never worked in programming before, that's okay! You should _still_ list your past roles so that hiring managers can at least see that:

- You have a history of being employed
- You have a history of being responsible and reliable
- You have a history of working with others

It's a lot better than _nothing_, and at some point _every professional programmer_ has to get their first programming job, so don't sweat it.

## Tech-Related Experience

If you have experience in QA, product management, IT Ops, or anything that's at least related to software development, update the descriptions of each of your past positions to _emphasize_ any part of your job that was related to software development. For example, for an IT Ops job, don't say this:

- Managed production environment upgrades
- Installed databases
- Analyzed logs to troubleshoot issues

Say this instead:

- Worked closely with the development team to ensure that the software was deployed and running smoothly.
- Wrote custom code to automate deployments.
- Built a SlackBot to provide real-time updates on the status of the production environment.

## Be Interesting

No matter what, try to make every line of text you publish (whether it's on LinkedIn, GitHub, or your resume) interesting and _specific_. It will attract more attention, garner follow-up questions, and generally just make you a more interesting person to work with. So don't say this:

- Worked on a backend REST API
- Worked on a collaborative team and practiced leadership skills
- Used Python and JavaScript to build an app that tracks data

Say this instead:

- I built a media uploading service using Kubernetes, Docker and Go that handled 1,000 files per second. It replaced the old service that struggled to handle 100 files per second.
- I led an initiative on my team to develop a plan to streamline our QA process for our React Native mobile app. We reduced the average turnaround time for a pull request approval by 50%.
- I built a full-stack Django app that pulls weather data in real-time and sends alerts to subscribed users in geographic areas at risk of severe weather.

## Assignment

**Fill out your LinkedIn experience section.**

Also, go _update your experience section on your resume_ to match if you made some meaningful improvements.

question.question: If you used to work as a landscaper, and are now trying to land your first programming job, you `____` include that landscaping experience in your work history.

title: Start Applying
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Start Applying

**It's time to start applying to jobs!**

You've got all you need to start sending cold applications to companies. There are additional strategies _aside_ from cold applying that we'll talk about soon, but I'm a believer in cold applying as well. It doesn't take a ton of time, and frankly, most of my jobs as a developer came from cold applying.

## Set a Weekly Goal

I sent around 150 applications over about an 8-week period before I landed my first full-time developer job.

**Pick a number of applications you want to send per week as a goal.** Make sure that it's high enough that you're getting yourself out there, but that it's low enough that you'll have the time to put in the _effort needed_ to make each application good.

As a rule of thumb:

- **Fewer than 10 applications** per week is likely too low
- **More than 50 applications** per week is likely too high

## Don't Wait

_Don't wait!_ There's no reason to put this off. The absolute worst thing that can happen when you submit an application is that they say no, or you don't hear back. You won't be insulted, you won't lose any money, and your credit score won't drop. _So there's no reason to wait_.

There are millions of companies out there, and **you only need one to say yes**.

Jonathan Hall says it well in this [~60-second clip](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BEMueuoQ4n8&t=2218s) from the Backend Banter podcast.

![Applying and networking](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/lesson_videos/networking-applying-1080x1920.mp4)

question.question: You should `____`
answers: wait until the end of this course to start applying
wait until a higher power gives you a sign that you're ready
start now! You're ready to start testing the waters
give up and keep doomscrolling on YouTube
question.correctAnswer: start now! You're ready to start testing the waters

title: Where to Apply
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Where to Apply

There are, of course, all the "normal" places to find jobs:

- Indeed
- LinkedIn
- AngelList
- ZipRecruiter
- Glassdoor

**Use them**, but they shouldn't be your _only_ source for open positions. _Everyone_ is applying to the top 50 jobs that show up when you type "backend developer" on LinkedIn. That doesn't mean you can't apply to those, but you should also be looking for other places to apply.

## Local Area

With the world more remote than ever, many candidates forget that there are _a lot_ of onsite-only jobs out there. Local, onsite jobs are **the best** for entry-level developers because:

1. The candidate pool (competition) is _much_ smaller than remote positions
2. You can meet the hiring managers in person - maybe even at a local tech meetup
3. You can get noticed by literally walking into the office and talking to the front desk
4. You can get a sense of the company culture by visiting the office
5. If you get the job, you'll be able to work with your coworkers and mentors in person, meaning you'll learn faster

## Company Websites

Most companies have a `/careers` page on their website. If they're hiring, find their positions there. By going directly to a company's website, it's more likely that you'll be applying to a job with a smaller candidate pool because the job may not be posted on one of the major job boards, or may simply not be coming to the top of the list when people search.

**Make a list of companies you'd like to work for, and check their websites regularly.**

## Niche Job Boards

There are smaller job boards out there that have far fewer applicants than the big ones. You'll want to find some on your own, but here are a few I've used in the past:

- [Levels.fyi](https://www.levels.fyi/jobs)
- [Golang cafe](https://golang.cafe/)
- [WeWorkRemotely](https://weworkremotely.com/)
- [Work at a Startup](https://www.workatastartup.com/jobs)
- [Hacker News](https://news.ycombinator.com/) monthly "Who is hiring?" and "Who wants to be hired?" threads
- Local Slack/Discord communities (don't spam general chats, look for a `#jobs` channel)

## Non-Tech Companies

I've noticed that a lot of developers get their "first programming job" at a _non_-tech company. These are companies that employ developers, but the product they sell isn't software. For example:

- A local marketing agency that needs a developer or two to build custom solutions for marketing analytics
- A manufacturing company might need developers to maintain a custom inventory management system
- An accounting firm that maintains a home-grown invoicing system

Jobs like this tend to _not pay quite as well_, but they can be a great stepping-stone job. You can use them to gain valuable experience, then move onto a sexy "tech" company later.

title: Side Doors
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Side Doors

There is a saying that goes something like:

> When the front door has a line around the block, find the side door.

![booth meme](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/bc0bC0E-388x300.png)

When you're trying to find places to apply, **look for side doors**. Openings that rank highly on the big job boards often have _hundreds_ of applicants, but there are other ways to get your foot in the door. For example:

- If you have a friend or family member that works at a company, ask for a referral.
- If you can find someone online that you _don't_ know that works at the company, ask them what it's like to work there and have a _real human_ conversation. If you hit it off, let them know you applied and ask them to mention your name.
- If you already work at a company that hires developers, but you're in a non-technical position, see if there are opportunities to transition into a developer role.
- Consistently engage on Twitter (X) with some of the employees at a company you admire. Build a relationship with them.
- Physically walk into the company's office with a paper copy of your resume and ask if you can drop it off with the hiring manager. This is bold, but at least it gets you noticed.

It's all about doing research on the company and digging around for some _non-annoying_ way to get more attention on your application.

## Not Every Application Needs a Side Door

I only recommend making the effort to find a side door on about 10% of your applications - the ones that you really, really want. If you try to do this for every application, you won't have time to apply to enough jobs.

question.question: If you only do the most obvious job search activities, you'll have `____`

title: Recruiters
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Recruiters

Third-party recruiters can be a fantastic way to land jobs, but they can _also_ be a huge waste of time.

If you're a highly sought-after senior developer with 6 years of experience, recruiters will bend over backward to get you hired because their commissions will be _fat_. Recruiters can make 30% of your yearly salary when they place you... but only if you're in-demand.

For your _first_ job, it's _less likely_ that a recruiter will be helpful.

## It Can Still Be Worthwhile

That said, it won't _hurt_ you to interact with a couple of recruiters if they reach out, and you might just get lucky.

Where you want to watch out is if they're asking you to compile all sorts of information, do homework projects, and hop on calls, and just end up ghosting you in the end. Recruiters often send bulk DMs on LinkedIn to anyone with the keyword "Python" in their profile, and then try to get any responders to do the work of eliminating themselves from the pool.

> [!lane]
> I recommend that you chat with recruiters if they reach out, and just see how it goes. At the very least it's a good learning experience, but if you get the feeling they're not serious and are wasting your time, don't be afraid to move on to other strategies.

title: Networking
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Networking

_Yuck_.

"Networking" sounds like a scummy word most often used by salespeople and wantrepreneurs... and it is. But it's _also_ a great way to get a job, though there are _bad_ ways to go about it.

Networking is the _ultimate_ side door. Having friends in the industry makes getting hired **much, much** easier.

## Just Make Friends

"Networking" is just corpo-speak for "making friends who share professional interests with you". If you think about it as this sleazy, transactional thing, you'll come off as, surprise, **sleazy and transactional**!

The goal is simply _to make friends_. If you apply your kindergarten teacher's advice on how to make friends, you'll be fine.

### Do Not:

- Cold DM people asking for work
- Join Discord servers and immediately spam the `#jobs` channel
- Show up at meetups and hand out business cards
- Send cold connection requests on LinkedIn saying you want to "network" with them

### Do:

- Engage in conversations about code with folks on X, LinkedIn, Slack, Discord, etc.
- Join coding communities and help others out with their problems
- Develop a reputation for being friendly at the local meetups
- Be funny, interesting, and helpful in all your interactions
- Get to know the companies in your local area and what they do

Make some friends. _This takes time_. Once you have some friends, you can ask them for help, or more likely, if they're aware of your goals, they'll _offer to help_ you when they see an opportunity. Networking is critical, but it's a **long-term game** that only pays dividends _after_ you've put in the time to build relationships.

question.question: Good networking is all about...
answers: providing value to others and making friends
letting everyone at the meetup know that `malloc()` is too high-level for your tastes
handing out business cards to everyone you meet
question.correctAnswer: providing value to others and making friends

title: In-Person Meetups
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# In-Person Meetups

Physical in-person tech meetups are _by far the best_ networking activity you can do. If you're _not_ going to a local meetup (given that one is available to you), I'd question your commitment to getting a job.

## Why Local Meetups Are So Good

- Meeting in person builds much stronger friendships than online chats
- They are almost always free
- Sometimes you get free food
- The people who attend meetups tend to be well-connected
- You will learn _a ton_ from the talks

**Check Meetup.com for local tech meetups and try to go to one each week**. Most meetups meet monthly in the evenings, so try to find several that you can attend regularly.

## What to Do at a Meetup

Individual meetup events usually work like this:

1. People trickle in before the talk starts (try to sit by someone and say hi!)
2. ~30-minute talk about a tech topic
3. People hang out and chat for ~30 minutes after the talk (this is the best time to meet people)

Your goal, again, should just be to get to know the people there. If you show up consistently, you'll figure out who the "regulars" are. Make sure you get to know them. Become a regular yourself. Make it known to the world that you're new to programming and you're looking for your first job, but don't ask favors of people you're just meeting for the first time. If they have advice or connections, they'll let you know once they get to know you better.

## Miriah Got Jobs from Meetups

In this [~60-second clip](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qpUC66QEA14&t=3791s) from the Backend Banter podcast, Miriah talks about how the majority of her jobs have come from either going to meetups or hosting them.

title: Virtual Meetups
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Virtual Meetups

If you're not near any physical meetups, you can still attend virtual meetups. These are usually done over Google Hangouts, Zoom, or something similar. While virtual meetups tend to be better than async discussions in a Slack channel, I want to be clear: **virtual meetups are _not_ as good as in-person meetups**.

But virtual is better than nothing. You'll still get to listen to talks and meet folks, but the connections won't be as powerful as they would be face-to-face. If you have the option to go to a physical meetup, _do that instead_.

title: Online Communities
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Online Communities

There are a lot of online communities out there for developers. Find two or three that you like and hang out in them regularly.

To be clear, you should _not_ go find 20 different Slack communities related to programming, join them all, and spam their "welcome" channel with messages about how you're looking for a job. Not only is that not going to work, but it's going to _get you blocked and banned as well_.

**I recommend three things universally**:

1. **Join (if you haven't already) the [Boot.dev Discord](https://boot.dev/community).** This is the best place for you to get general-purpose job search help from our community.
2. **Join the official Discord/Slack/Forum for the programming language or framework** you're most interested in working with professionally. For example, the [Golang Slack](https://invite.slack.golangbridge.org/) or the [Python Discord](https://discord.gg/python).
3. **Join a Discord or Slack group for the tech scene in your local area**.

You will probably only have enough time to meaningfully connect with folks in a few communities, so don't go overboard. As with physical meetups, the point here is to become a "regular" and get to know people. If you're a regular, and you make some friends, they'll be much more likely to help you out when an opportunity arises.

**Offer help more often than you ask for it**.

Cryptocurrency, Pump and Dump, and AI grifting

title: Social Media
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Social Media

I see a lot of folks spend a bunch of time on X, LinkedIn, and other social platforms trying to find job opportunities by writing posts, replying to posts, and DMing people.

If you were going to waste that time doomscrolling X anyway, then sure, maybe these sorts of activities are a productivity improvement... but compared to _other things you could be doing_, it's not the best use of time.

Having a good LinkedIn profile is important, but sending random connection requests to people in your feed to "grow your network" isn't. X can be a good place to learn about what's going on in the industry, but it's just not a good time investment for _most_ people.

> [!lane]
> Social is only worth your time if you make absolute banger posts. If you write bangers and grow a following, it can help. But most of us don't write bangers... ~~My views have been rough lately don't judge~~.

_Spend time on social media at your own risk._ Prefer in-person networking, virtual meetups, and tight-knit communities over the content grind.

# Interviews

If you send 100 applications, you might get 10 phone screens, 5 interviews, and 1 offer. Those numbers are just estimates, but hopefully, it demonstrates that:

1. You need to send a lot of applications
2. You need to do a lot of interviews

Interviewing is a skill, and it's one that you can get better at with practice. My goals with this section are to help you get started on the right foot and to give you some pointers that will help you improve your interviewing skills _faster_.

![Interviewing tips](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/lesson_videos/interviewing-tips-1080x1920.mp4)

## Listen to This Episode

You don't have to do this now, but I highly recommend listening to this [full episode from the Backend Banter](https://www.backendbanter.fm/episodes/013-landing-your-first-coding-job-with-james-q-quick) podcast at some point when you have free time. It's available on YouTube or any major podcasting app, and is just packed with advice for getting your first job as a developer from James Q Quick.

title: Relax
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Relax

I know, just telling you to relax when you're sitting in an interview isn't going to make you relax, but... try? If you're relaxed, you'll pay attention more easily, you'll recall information more easily, and you'll perform better in general. Here are a few things to keep in mind that should help you relax in interviews:

### 1. You Probably Aren't Getting This Job

Everyone interviews at _way_ more companies throughout their careers than they'll ever work for. I've personally only had 4 programming jobs, but I've been an interviewee at least 50 times.

It's just a numbers game. Don't get too attached to the idea of working for the company that you're interviewing with. If you do, the pressure will be on, and you'll be more likely to make mistakes.

Instead, just think of it as a conversation. You're just getting to know each other. By all means, show _enthusiasm_ for the company, but go into the interview with a mindset of connecting with the interviewers, practicing your interviewing skills, and learning a bit about the professional world of software development.

### 2. It's Not a Waste of Time

After an interview, if you find out that you didn't get the job, the time spent was _not_ wasted. In fact, it was super valuable practice that you got _for free_.

1. You'll get practice solving technical problems and communicating your solutions
2. You'll learn what more interview processes look like in the real world
3. You'll learn about the company and what they do with their software
4. You'll learn about various tech stacks used in the industry, and which ones you need to study up on
5. You'll figure out some of your own weaknesses and knowledge gaps that you can work on for the next interview

### 3. You'll Get More Chances

This isn't your one audition for America's Got Talent. If you don't make it, there are hundreds of other interviews to be conducted. It's most important that you're getting out there consistently and practicing your interview skills.

The better a job you do with your applications, the more interviews you get. The more interviews you have, the less pressure you'll feel to nail the one you're in.

question.question: Which is the most practical way to be more relaxed in interviews?
answers: Do a lot of interviews so that you're used to the process and each one doesn't feel so high-stakes
question.correctAnswer: Do a lot of interviews so that you're used to the process and each one doesn't feel so high-stakes

title: Confidence
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Confidence

Most new developers don't have _nearly_ enough confidence in themselves during a coding interview, and it shows. I know it's not helpful to just tell you to be confident, but it is something that you can improve on with some effort and preparation.

## Recognize How Far You've Come

Boot.dev is all about learning to code _the long way_. We don't take shortcuts. If you've made it this far and built all those projects, you've learned _a lot_. You've probably learned more about the practical skills used in development than most 4-year CS graduates, and more about computer science theory than Bootcamp grads and other self-taught developers.

## Companies Don't Hire People, People Hire People

Companies don't think for themselves. Companies are made up of _people_, and the people in a company don't belong to a singular hive mind. They don't do exactly what's in the company's best interest at all times. The people in a company are loosely aligned in their mission to make the company money, but each person has their own wants, needs, pains and incentives.

People hire people who solve _their_ problems. They're not always looking for the absolute best developer, they're looking for a coworker that will make their job and life easier. They want someone who will:

- Save them time
- Make them look good
- Build out their team, giving them more authority and influence
- Deliver the project that will secure their next promotion
- Be fun to be around

Your interviewer will wonder:

- Will I have to hold your hand constantly?
- Will you learn the codebase and be able to contribute quickly?
- Do you bring new skills to the team?
- Will you be able to quickly learn the things you need to know?
- Will hiring you make me look good?

## Don't Be a Charity Case

Avoid any sob stories or self-deprecating comments. I've conducted interviews where the candidate said things like:

> I'm not very good at algorithms, I didn't have money for college

> I've been applying for 2 years and I haven't gotten any offers yet

My best guess is that these candidates thought that we would feel sorry and pity-offer them a job. _That's not going to happen._ It's not because we don't care, or because we're ruthless, but it would simply be _unethical_ to hire the less qualified person because they had the sadder (and impossible to verify) story.

You need to show off that you can solve problems and that you're enjoyable to work with. That does _not_ mean you need to know everything, but it does mean you should present the best possible version of yourself.

title: Humility
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Humility

I know I just said you need to be confident in your abilities, and you _do_. You need to:

- **Be confident** in your ability to learn, communicate, solve problems, and build solutions
- **Be humble** about your current working set of knowledge

You're new to this, and that's okay! You don't know everything there is to know about programming, _no one does_. There's just too much to know, and your interviewers are aware of this.

## Humble Folks Are Fun to Work With

Remember, a human is hiring you, and this human will have to work with you daily if they hire you. From a high level, they want 2 things:

1. You can do the job well so they don't have to hold your hand
2. You're enjoyable to work with so they don't dread coming to work

- Know-it-alls are no fun to work with. They're arrogant, they don't listen, and they're not open to new ideas
- Being humble communicates that you will be teachable and learn faster
- Being humble means you're more likely to be willing to adapt to how the team does things

question.question: Decision makers want to hire folks that...
answers: Will make their work lives easier and more enjoyable
Have the most experience on paper
question.correctAnswer: Will make their work lives easier and more enjoyable

title: Structure
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Structure

The structure of your interview process will vary from company to company, but there are a few common steps that I can forewarn you about:

1. The phone screen
2. The homework assignment
3. The cultural interview
4. The technical interview
5. The offer

Larger companies tend to have more structure and longer processes. Small companies tend to have less structure and shorter processes.

## 1. The Phone Screen

If you're getting called, this means that your resume was chosen out of a big stack! Congratulations! You're already doing something right. This isn't an interview, it's just a phone call to get to know you a bit and to see if you're worth bringing in for an interview.

Sometimes this is done by a hiring manager (small company) but is often done by a recruiter or HR person (larger company). They'll ask you a few questions about your background, and they'll tell you a bit about the company and the position. This is a great time to ask questions about the company, the position, and the rest of the interview process.

_It's unlikely you'll be disqualified at this stage._ The only reason I've disqualified candidates here in the past is because they were rude or there was a misunderstanding about the information on their resume.

## 2. The Homework Assignment

Not all companies send homework assignments, but some do, and it tends to be more common for junior or entry-level positions. Hopefully, it goes without saying, but do your best on any homework assignments you undertake. This is a stage where some percentage (maybe even a majority) of candidates are disqualified.

In descending order of importance:

1. Complete the task
2. Make sure your code is readable and well-organized
3. Make it performant

If you're in the fortunate position of having many companies wanting you to do homework assignments, be judicious. Don't do a bad job on 5 take-home assignments. Do a good job on 2-3 and either turn down the rest or ask for more time.

_Be quick._ Use the full time given (it's common to be told not to spend more than X hours). But complete it and turn it in quickly. This moves you to the top of the pile and shows that you're excited and motivated.

## 3. The Cultural Interview

Some companies combine the cultural (soft skills) interview and the technical (hard skills) interview, but some separate them, or even have multiples of each.

This part of the interview won't contain tricky technical problems, it's really just:

- Do we like this person?
- Will we get along with them?
- Will they fit in with the team?
- Are they a good communicator?
- Are they happy? Or a downer?
- Hard worker? Responsible? Kind?

I don't have much more advice here, other than to relax and try to make a connection with the interviewer. Take interest in them, ask them questions, and try to find common ground. Classic stuff from the book "How to Win Friends and Influence People".

## 4. The Technical Interview

There are several main categories of technical interviews:

- Whiteboarding (drawing + talking)
- Verbal problem solving (talking through a problem)
- Live coding challenges (coding on a shared screen or in-person)
- Walkthroughs of past projects (explaining code you've written in the past)

Of these, the _content_ of the questions usually falls into one of these categories:

- **Data structures and algorithms:** Write an algorithm that finds matching tweets in `log(n)` time.
- **System design and architecture:** Which endpoints would you expose in a REST API for a URL shortener?
- **Problem-solving and debugging:** Here's a bug; explain your approach to solving it.
- **Language- or technology-specific questions:** What's the difference between a slice and an array in Go?

> [!lane]
> In my experience, larger companies tend to do more whiteboarding and live coding, while smaller companies tend to place more emphasis on verbal problem-solving and walkthroughs of past projects.

## 5. The Offer

If you get an offer, congratulations! The one piece of advice I'll give here is to take a couple of days to "think" about it. You don't need to accept immediately. Ideally, you can get a second offer and make a choice, but even if you don't, take a day or two.

Your first job doesn't need to be the sexiest high-paying job in the world. Sometimes it makes sense to take the base hit and use the experience to leverage your way into a better spot in a couple of years. Of course, if you're in a position to be picky, then by all means, be picky, but if you're in super high demand you probably don't need my advice anyway.

title: Be on the Team
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Be on the Team

One of the best tactics for a technical interview is to reframe the situation in your mind.

**Rather than thinking of it as an _exam_, think of it as a _collaboration_.** Generally, you're not being left high and dry by your interviewer, you're able to ask clarifying questions and _suggest_ solutions rather than _provide_ them. Let me show you some examples.

## Bad Example

> **Interviewer:** "Write a function that accepts a string and returns the string reversed."
>
> **You:** "Ok"
>
> **You:** _writes function_
>
> **You:** "Here you go"

## Good Example

> **Interviewer:** "Write a function that accepts a string and returns the string reversed."
>
> **You:** "Got it, how concerned about memory are we? Should I try to reverse the string in place, or should I create a new string?"
>
> **Interviewer:** "Let's say we're not concerned about memory, we just want a solution that works."
>
> **You:** _writes signature of function_
>
> **You:** "Ok, so we just want a single string in and a single string out... like this, right?"
>
> **Interviewer:** "Yep!"
>
> **You, _while writing the rest of the function_:** "Ok, so I'll just create a new string, loop through the input string backwards, and add each character to the new string.
> This should handle cases of empty strings automatically."

## Key Differences

The second example is better for many reasons:

1. You're asking clarifying questions as you go, which does two things:
   - It shows that you'll ask clarifying questions on the job, which is a _very_ good trait. You'd be surprised how many developers spend days or weeks building the wrong thing because they misunderstood the requirements.
   - You'll get answers in real time, which means you're more likely to build the correct solution.
2. You're communicating your thought process:
   - Your interviewer can (and often will) actually help you if you start going down the wrong path.
   - It shows your interviewer how you think, which provides more _signal_ about your abilities.
   - It shows that you're able to think through edge cases, something you'll need to do on the job.
   - It shows that you're a great communicator, which is critical to being a good developer.
   - It breaks up the awkward silence which, frankly, no one is super comfortable with.
3. You're using words like "we" and "us" instead of "you" and "me":
   - This subliminally signals to the interviewer that you're already part of the team.
   - It's friendly and collaborative language, which just makes you more likable and lightens the mood.

title: Ask Questions
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Ask Questions

Asking questions makes you look smart. Smart people ask lots of (good) questions. They're knowledge sponges. They want to learn as much as they can. People want to work with smart people.

## Turn "I Don't Know" Into a Conversation

Try to expound on "I don't know"s. If you don't have a textbook answer, try to explain what you _do_ know, or mention a related topic that you know about.

### Bad Example

> Interviewer: "What is a closure, and when would you use one?"

> You: "I think it has to do with functional programming, but I don't know"

### Good Example

> Interviewer: "What is a closure, and when would you use one?"

> You: "I learned about closures when I took a course on functional programming, but I don't remember every detail... I do remember using them in my link aggregator project. Did it have something to do with functions and state?"

In the second example you accomplish a few things:

1. You communicate that you at least have _partial_ knowledge, which is better than none.
2. You keep the conversation moving, which is better than an awkward dead end.
3. You ask a question, which often will actually get you the hint you need to go on and give a good answer.

## Clarify, Clarify, and Clarify

In the real world, the best developers ask a lot of clarifying questions to their product managers, designers, and engineering leaders. It ensures they don't waste everyone's time building the wrong things. Here's a good example:

> Interviewer: "Let's say you're designing a CRUD API to manage users and their photos. How would you design the endpoints?"

> You: (after a ~5 second pause) "Hmm... are users allowed to share photos?"

> Interviewer: "Good question... let's assume that users can share photos, but there is still a primary owner of each photo."

> You: "Okay, well then I would probably have a `/users` endpoint to get information about specific users, and I would have a `/users/:id/photos` endpoint to get all the photos associated with each user. Would that work for our usecase?"

title: Prepare
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Prepare

Once you've landed an interview, it makes a lot of sense to take an hour or two for some dedicated preparation. I'd recommend doing the following for every single interview that you're serious about:

1. **Research the product/service.** Make sure you know what the company offers and who their customers are.
2. **Research the tech they use.** Make sure you at least know the flashcard-level definitions of all the programming languages, frameworks, and databases they work with.
3. **Research the team.** If possible, figure out who will be conducting the interview, and do some digital sleuthing to learn a bit more about them. If they post online about work, read their posts!

If you show up to an interview and are asked, "What do you know about MySQL?" and your response is "nothing," you're going to look unprepared and uninterested. _Especially_ if MySQL was listed in the job description or you could have easily found out that they use it with a few minutes of research.

This doesn't mean you need to go learn everything about MySQL before the interview if you've never used it before, but you should at least know:

- It's a relational SQL database
- It's open source
- It's similar to PostgreSQL and MS SQL Server

_Just learn a few high-level details so that when you're asked about it, you don't look like a deer in the headlights._

answers: technical writings of the members of the team you'll be joining
