title: Practice
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Practice

I know we talked about this earlier, but it's worth repeating now: **don't stop learning and building while you're job-searching**.

As you get deeper into the job search, your chances of getting hired should _increase_ not _decrease_. Part of that will be due to your interviewing and outreach skills improving, but your raw technical skills should also be improving.

In order of importance, I'd spend time:

1. Improving your capstone project(s)
2. Completing optional deeper learning courses on Boot.dev
3. Practicing your coding challenge skills in the [Training Grounds](https://www.boot.dev/training)

## Take Notes

At the end of the day, **spend your practice time on whatever you feel like you're struggling with the most**. After leaving an interview, jot down notes about any of the questions that gave you difficulty. Then spend some time practicing and reviewing that content.

**Don't get stumped by the same technical challenge twice!**

Of course, you won't know what your weaknesses are until you do some interviews! _So get out there and start interviewing_.

question.question: What should you spend your time practicing? (choose the best answer)
answers: Whatever you're struggling with in the interviews you're getting
question.correctAnswer: Whatever you're struggling with in the interviews you're getting

title: Relocation
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Relocation

**It's usually easier to land in-person jobs than remote jobs as a junior developer.** As you gain more experience, you'll have more opportunities to work remotely. For your first job I recommend mostly thinking about in-person jobs.

> [!lane]
> I relocated from a small town in Southern Utah to Salt Lake City for my first job, and it was one of the best decisions I've ever made.

## Why Relocate?

If you're in a small town or non-tech hub:

1. There probably aren't many developer jobs.
2. The jobs that exist likely aren't the best.

That doesn't mean you shouldn't look locally first, especially if you're not keen on moving. But if after a few months you feel that greener pastures exist, relocation can be a great option.

> [!tip]
> Just a couple lifetimes ago people literally walked across continents in search of better opportunities. I'm not saying that it's not harder for some people than others, but the world is a big place, and technologically speaking it's never been easier to pick up your life and move somewhere else. **Don't let small excuses hold you back.**

question.question: It is usually `____` and `____` to get an onsite job than a remote job as a junior dev.

title: When to Relocate
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# When to Relocate

Even if you're willing to relocate, I'd still recommend doing research and starting your job search _long before_ you start packing your bags. I'd probably have a plan that looks like this:

1. Apply to local companies first.
2. If that doesn't work, start applying to tech hubs. Be clear in your applications that you're willing to relocate, and don't point out that you're not currently living there unless explicitly asked.
3. If that doesn't work, consider moving even before you have an offer and continue to apply once you get there.

Companies tend to be wary of hiring people who need to relocate for a few reasons:

- They don't want to pay for your relocation.
- If they need to fire you, they feel worse about it because you uprooted your life.
- It's harder to conduct interviews, assuming they prefer in-person interviews.

It's always easier to get a job in a city where you already live. You can drive over for the interview. The company doesn't have to pay or wait for you to relocate. You can start sooner. _It's just easier_.

The trouble is, of course, that it's risky to move to a new city without a job offer in hand. You might have to work a non-tech job to make ends meet during your job search.

To be clear: **I'm not telling you what to do here, I just want you to consider the pros and cons of relocation.**

title: Is It the Location?
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# Is It the Location?

If you're having trouble finding work, that doesn't necessarily _mean_ that it's your location that's the problem... but it might be. I've talked to _hundreds_ of aspiring developers who _drastically_ misdiagnose their job search problems.

- I met a woman with an awful resume who was convinced that Los Angeles just had no opportunities.
- I met a guy at a meetup who had never heard of Postgres who was convinced that AI had taken all the jobs
- I met someone on Twitter claiming that they were too old at 28 to break into tech. When I asked for their portfolio they didn't have a single project on GitHub ready to share.

So, before feeling like you _must_ relocate, get really introspective on a few things:

1. Does your portfolio look fantastic?
2. Are your LinkedIn, resume, and GitHub profiles in a great state?
3. Have you applied to all the jobs you can find in your area, and have you tried any side doors?
4. If you're getting interviews, are you actually doing well in them?

As with many things in life, a great strategy is to start checking boxes on everything you can easily control. Make sure that none of those things are the bigger problems before you start thinking about riskier options.

question.question: Moving to a tech-hub can make landing a job easier, but location might not be your biggest obstacle

title: It's Your Life
courseTitle: Learn How to Find a Programming Job
readme:
# It's Your Life

If you're single and have no dependents, then you can probably survive in a new city with a random landscaping, janitorial, or Starbucks job. The risk is relatively low, and the reward is potentially high.

However, if you have 4 kids, a mortgage, and a job at the local high school, it can be incredibly risky to move without an offer in hand.

**Do what you think is best for you and your family.**

answers: It depends on your personal situation
question.correctAnswer: It depends on your personal situation
